name: Ame-no-Uzume
description: Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto, often shortened to Ame-no-Uzume, is the goddess of dawn, mirth, revelry, and performing arts in Japanese Shinto mythology. She is renowned for her pivotal role in coaxing the sun goddess Amaterasu out of the Ama-no-Iwato (heavenly rock cave), thereby restoring light to the world. Her name can be translated as 'Heavenly Alarming Female.' Ame-no-Uzume achieved this feat through a boisterous and suggestive dance, which captivated the other deities and made Amaterasu curious enough to peek out, ultimately being drawn out completely. She is considered a foundational figure for kagura dance and theatre, and is often depicted with a mischievous and joyous expression. Her actions represent the power of laughter, performance, and celebration to overcome darkness and despair. She is a symbol of female empowerment, resilience, and the importance of joy in the face of adversity. Ame-no-Uzume continues to be revered in Shinto shrines and festivals, where her dances are often reenacted to honor her contribution to the world and to ensure continued prosperity and happiness. She is believed to bring good fortune and protect against evil spirits.
subtitle: Goddess of Dawn, Mirth, and Dance

keyMyths.title: Amaterasu's Cave
keyMyths.description: Ame-no-Uzume performs a provocative dance to lure Amaterasu out of the rock cave, bringing light back to the world. Her dance is central to the myth.

keyMyths.title: Marriage to Sarutahiko
keyMyths.description: After the Amaterasu incident, Ame-no-Uzume marries Sarutahiko, the leader of the earthly kami, solidifying the connection between heaven and earth. This union is significant.

keyMyths.title: Origin of Kagura
keyMyths.description: Ame-no-Uzume's dance is considered the origin of Kagura, a traditional Japanese performing art often dedicated to the kami. It is a sacred tradition.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: The earliest written record of Japanese mythology.
sources.text: The earliest written record of Japanese mythology.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: Another early and influential text on Japanese history and mythology.
